name: Nytorv 2 — Birthplace
description: Søren Kierkegaard was born here on 5 May 1813. His father Michael Pedersen Kierkegaard, a prosperous hosier, owned the house on the corner of Nytorv and Gammeltorv. The building is now a Danske Bank branch; a small grey plaque marks the spot.

name: Vor Frue Kirke — Cathedral
description: The Church of Our Lady, where Kierkegaard's family worshipped and where his funeral was held on 18 November 1855. Bishop Mynster, whom Kierkegaard attacked in his final polemic, preached here. Bertel Thorvaldsen's statue of Christ stands at the altar.

name: Borgerdydskolen — School
description: Kierkegaard attended this school in Klareboderne from 1821 to 1830, where he studied Latin, Greek, and history. He was described as very conservative and frequently got into altercations with fellow students.

name: Københavns Universitet
description: Kierkegaard enrolled in 1830 and defended his dissertation On the Concept of Irony here in 1841. The main building fronts on Frue Plads, directly across from the cathedral.

name: Nørregade — Apartments
description: Kierkegaard lived at several addresses on and near Nørregade over the course of his life, including at number 230A (later renumbered). He famously kept his domestic arrangements modest while spending lavishly on carriage rides and café visits.

name: Regensen — Student Residence
description: The historic student residence at Store Kannikestræde, across from Rundetårn, part of the university milieu Kierkegaard inhabited during his long student years in the 1830s. The college has stood here since 1623.

name: Det Kongelige Teater — Royal Theatre
description: The theatre on Kongens Nytorv represents the aesthetic stage of existence in Kierkegaard's thought. It was also central to the Corsair affair: after Kierkegaard provoked the satirical journal, it published caricatures of him that made him a figure of public ridicule from 1845 onward.

name: Kongens Have — King's Garden
description: Kierkegaard was a habitual walker and was frequently seen in this park beside Rosenborg Castle. His street-level encounters with ordinary Copenhageners — servants, laborers, strangers — informed his concept of indirect communication.

name: Trinitatis Kirke / Rundetårn
description: The Trinity Church and Round Tower complex on Købmagergade. Kierkegaard attended services here. The tower — an observatory ascended by a long spiral ramp rather than stairs — is an apt emblem of his circling, indirect method of approach.

name: Assistens Kirkegård — Grave
description: Kierkegaard's final resting place in Nørrebro. He died on 11 November 1855, aged 42, having collapsed in the street weeks earlier. Hans Christian Andersen and Niels Bohr are also buried here. The cemetery doubles as a park — fitting for a philosopher who loved to walk.

name: Rosenborggade 7–9 — Two Apartments
description: Two adjacent buildings where Kierkegaard lived from 1848 to 1850. He first took an apartment in the corner building at No. 9 (Tornebuskegade 1), writing in his journal that he had been in love with it since the day it was built. But master tanner Johan Julius Gram's yard was behind the building, and the smell proved unbearable: he wrote that he had to "almost use mental capacity on not becoming sick from impatience." In October 1848 he moved to the first floor of No. 7 next door — though the tanner's biological waste in the gutter meant little improvement. A plaque on No. 7 records that he wrote The Sickness unto Death and Practice in Christianity here, along with substantial parts of The Point of View of My Work as an Author and The Lily of the Field and the Bird of the Air. He finally left for Nørregade 35 in April 1850.

name: Østerbrogade 108A — Outside the Walls
description: The only address Kierkegaard ever kept outside the old city walls. He lived here from April 1851 to October 1852 — an unusual move for a man who rarely strayed far from the center of Copenhagen and never left Denmark after his final trip to Berlin in 1846.

name: Frederiks Hospital — Death
description: Kierkegaard collapsed in the street on 2 October 1855 and was admitted here on Bredgade with paralysis in both legs. He refused communion from a pastor — insisting that the established church had to be abandoned — and died on 11 November 1855, aged 42. The building now houses a design museum.

name: Rosengården 10 — Rasmus Nielsen
description: From 1838 to 1841, the theologian and philosopher Rasmus Nielsen (1809–1884) lived here. Nielsen completed his theological doctorate in 1840 and became a professor at the University of Copenhagen in 1841. In the early 1840s he became acquainted with Kierkegaard, and the two maintained a complex intellectual relationship. Nielsen was initially drawn to Hegelian idealism before later aligning with Grundtvigianism.
